template_hint: Greet the parents warmly and reassure them it's a delicate topic, acknowledge that kids are naturally energetic, describe the specific damage and the safety and cost concerns, make a gentle request for closer supervision with reasons, suggest a constructive solution, and reaffirm the friendship.
sample_response_band12: Hi Anna, Raj, do you have a minute? Please don't take this the wrong way, because I really value us being friends and the kids are lovely. I've been meaning to mention something a bit awkward. Lately there's been some damage around the building, a couple of hallway lights broken, dents in the elevator doors, and a few scratches on cars in the lot, and it seems to happen when the little ones are out playing. I know how energetic kids are, honestly. But the repair costs get split among all of us, and I'd hate for anyone to actually get hurt. Do you think you could keep a closer eye on them in the common areas, or maybe set a few boundaries about where they play? Thanks so much for hearing me out. I really appreciate you both.

title: Roommate Who Pays Bills Late
prompt_text: Your roommate is responsible for paying half of the rent and utilities, but for the past three months they have paid you their share days or even weeks late. This has forced you to cover the full amount yourself and you are starting to fall behind. Talk to your roommate, explain how the late payments are affecting you, and ask them to start paying on time.
connector_phrases: I hate to bring this up, but
I just need us to be on the same page
template_hint: Greet the roommate warmly, gently raise the late-payment issue, explain how covering the full amount is hurting you, request a firm payment date with reasons, propose a simple solution like an automatic transfer, and reassure them you value living together.
sample_response_band12: Hey, do you have a minute? I wanted to talk about the bills, and I hope this doesn't come across the wrong way. The thing is, for the last few months your half has been arriving pretty late, so I've ended up covering the full rent and utilities on my own. Honestly, that's left me really tight on cash, and last month I almost missed a payment myself. I'm not upset at all, I just need us to be on the same page. Would it be possible for you to send me your share by the first of each month? Maybe we could even set up an automatic transfer so neither of us has to think about it. That way nobody falls behind and there's no awkwardness. I really like living with you, so I'd rather sort this out now before it becomes a bigger deal. Does that work?

title: Messy Shared Living Spaces
prompt_text: Your roommate consistently leaves the kitchen, living room, and bathroom messy, with dirty dishes piling up and clutter everywhere. You have been quietly cleaning up after them, but it is becoming exhausting and you feel taken for granted. Speak to your roommate, raise the issue politely, and try to agree on a fair way to keep the shared areas clean.
connector_phrases: I don't want to nag, but
It would mean a lot to me if
I'm sure we can figure something out
template_hint: Open in a friendly way, acknowledge it's an awkward topic, describe the mess without blaming, explain how cleaning up alone is wearing you out, propose a shared chore schedule, and end by reassuring them you want a comfortable home for both of you.
sample_response_band12: Hey, can we chat for a sec? I don't want to come across as nagging, because I really do enjoy living with you. But I've been wanting to talk about the shared spaces for a while. Lately the dishes and clutter have been piling up in the kitchen and bathroom, and honestly I've been the one tidying it all up. I'm getting a bit worn out, and I'd hate to start feeling resentful when there's such an easy fix. So how about we set up a simple chore schedule, where we take turns with the dishes and a quick weekly clean? Even just rinsing your plate right after dinner would make a big difference. It would mean a lot to me if we shared the load more evenly. I'm sure we can figure something out that works for both of us. Sound fair?

title: Partner Always Staying Over
prompt_text: Your roommate's partner has been staying over almost every night, and it now feels like you are living with a third person who does not pay rent but uses the utilities and shared spaces. You value your privacy, and the arrangement is starting to feel unfair to you. Talk to your roommate, raise your concerns honestly, and propose a reasonable limit or compromise.
connector_phrases: Please don't take this personally, but
I just want us to find a balance
template_hint: Approach calmly and stress you like their partner, raise the frequency of overnight stays, explain the impact on your privacy and shared costs, request a reasonable limit, offer a compromise such as set nights or splitting utilities, and reassure them you don't want any tension.
sample_response_band12: Hey, can we talk for a minute? Please don't take this the wrong way, because I genuinely like your partner and I'm glad you're happy. The thing is, they've been staying over almost every night lately, and it's started to feel like we have a third roommate. I really value my personal space, and honestly the utility bills have crept up too. I'm not asking them to never come over, of course. Would you be open to maybe a few nights a week, and the rest of the time we keep it just the two of us? Or if they're going to stay a lot, maybe they could chip in toward the bills. I completely get it if this is a sensitive thing to bring up. I just want us to find a balance that feels fair, so there's no tension between us. What do you think?

title: Eating My Food Without Asking
prompt_text: Your roommate keeps eating the groceries you buy with your own money, often finishing things you were saving for meals, without ever asking or replacing them. You have started to feel frustrated every time you open the fridge. Speak to your roommate, bring this up without starting an argument, and suggest a fair way to handle shared and personal food.
connector_phrases: I just want to keep things easy between us
template_hint: Start lightly so it doesn't feel like an attack, name the food issue gently, explain your frustration without exaggerating, request that they ask first or replace items, propose separate shelves or a shared grocery fund, and close on a friendly note.
sample_response_band12: Hey, can I bring up something quick? This might sound a little petty, but it's been bugging me and I'd rather just say it than let it build up. I've noticed some of my groceries keep disappearing, like the leftovers I was saving for lunch the other day. Honestly, it's a small thing, but it adds up when I have to keep re-buying stuff. So I was thinking, maybe we could just give each other a heads-up before grabbing the other person's food, and replace it afterward. Or, if it's easier, we could split the fridge into separate shelves, or even start a little shared grocery fund for the basics we both use. No big deal at all, I just want to keep things easy and friendly between us. Does that sound okay to you?

title: Declining To Host Holiday Dinner
prompt_text: Every year you host the big holiday dinner for your entire family at your home, and everyone expects you to do it again. This year, however, you are not able to host because of your workload, health, or other commitments. Speak to a family member who is expecting the usual gathering at your place. Explain that you cannot host this year and suggest another arrangement.
vocabulary_tips: not up to it this year
connector_phrases: I know everyone counts on us for this, and
I feel terrible about it, but
I just can't take it on this year
I was hoping we could find another way
What if we tried something different
I still want us all together, of course
template_hint: Open warmly about the tradition, admit you can't host this year and why, then propose a concrete alternative so the celebration still happens.
sample_response_band12: Hey Aunt Carol, I need to talk to you about the holiday dinner, and honestly, I feel a little guilty bringing it up. I know everyone counts on us hosting at our place every year, and it's become such a lovely tradition. The thing is, this year has been overwhelming with work and a few health issues, and I just can't take it on the way I usually do. I'm so sorry to change things up. But I really don't want us to miss out on being together. So I was thinking, maybe we could rotate this year and host it at your place, and I'd happily handle the desserts and a couple of dishes. Or we could do a potluck so nobody's stuck with all the work. The most important thing to me is that the whole family is together, laughing around the table. Can we figure out a plan that works?

title: Disputing The Damaged Deposit
prompt_text: You are moving out, and the landlord is withholding part of the security deposit because of damage to a wall and the carpet. You believe most of that damage was caused by your roommate, but your roommate insists the cost should be split fifty-fifty. You can either accept an even split to keep the peace, or push for your roommate to pay the larger share because they caused most of the damage. Decide which option you will take, and explain your decision to your roommate.
connector_phrases: I really don't want this to cause a rift, but
I hope you can see where I'm coming from
Let's settle this and move on
template_hint: Address the roommate calmly, acknowledge the situation is awkward and you value the friendship, lay out the facts about the damage, state your decision clearly with reasons, offer a fair compromise so it doesn't feel one-sided, and close by stressing you want to part on good terms.
sample_response_band12: Hey, I know this whole deposit thing is awkward, and the last thing I want is for it to cause a rift between us. But I do need to be honest. From my side, the wall and the carpet damage mostly happened in your room and from your stuff, so a fifty-fifty split doesn't feel fair to me. I'd love to just keep the peace, but I'd basically be paying for damage I didn't cause. So here's what I'd suggest as a compromise: let's cover the small shared wear and tear evenly, but you take on the larger portion for the carpet and wall. To be fair, I'm happy to put it all in writing so it's clear for both of us. I really hope you can see where I'm coming from. Let's settle this fairly and part on good terms.

title: Roommate Breaking The Lease Early
prompt_text: Your roommate has just told you they want to move out three months early and break your shared lease so they can take a job in another city. This would leave you on the hook for the full rent or scrambling to find a replacement on your own. You can either ask them to keep paying their share until a new roommate is found, or ask them to help you find and screen a replacement before they leave. Choose one option and explain it to your roommate while keeping the friendship intact.
connector_phrases: I'm genuinely happy for you, but
Here's the situation on my end
I don't think it's fair for me to
I'm sure we can work this out together
template_hint: Congratulate them sincerely, acknowledge the tough spot it leaves you in, explain why you can't cover everything alone, state your chosen request with clear reasons, propose how you'll work together on it, and reassure them you support their move and want to stay friends.
sample_response_band12: Hey, first of all, congratulations on the new job, I'm genuinely happy for you, that's a huge opportunity. But I do need to be honest about where it leaves me, because breaking the lease three months early puts me on the hook for the full rent, and there's no way I can cover that on my own. So here's what I'd really appreciate. Before you leave, would you be willing to help me find and screen a solid replacement? You know the place and what we're looking for, so between the two of us we could probably fill the room quickly. And if there's any gap before someone moves in, I'd ask that you cover your share until then. I don't think it's fair for me to carry the whole cost for a decision I wasn't part of. I'm sure we can work this out together, and I really want us to stay friends through it.

title: Declining An Expensive Trip
prompt_text: A close friend is excited about an expensive international trip and really wants you to come along, but the cost is well beyond what you can afford right now. You don't want to lie about your reasons, but you also don't want to hurt the friendship or dampen your friend's excitement. Call your friend, decline the trip honestly, and suggest a more affordable way to spend time together.
connector_phrases: I was so excited when you asked, but
It's just not the right time for me
template_hint: Open by showing genuine excitement about the invitation, acknowledge that this is hard to say, explain honestly that it's a budget issue, decline clearly, propose a more affordable alternative to spend time together, and reassure them you still want to be part of their life.
sample_response_band12: Hey Priya, I was honestly so excited when you asked me to come on this trip, it sounds incredible. But I have to be straight with you, and this isn't easy to say. The honest truth is the cost is just out of my budget right now. With everything going on financially, a trip like this would really stretch me, and I don't want to spend the whole time stressed about money. So I'm going to have to sit this one out, and I'm genuinely sad about it. But here's what I'd love instead: when you're back, let's plan a weekend road trip somewhere closer, just the two of us, something I can actually afford. And I want to hear every single story. Please don't think I'm not happy for you, because I really am. Go and have the best time, okay?

title: Addressing A Chronically Late Friend
prompt_text: A good friend is constantly late whenever you make plans, and lately it has started to frustrate you and waste a lot of your time. You value the friendship, but you can't keep ignoring the problem. You can either raise it directly and ask them to change, or tell them you'll start making backup plans so their lateness no longer leaves you stuck. Decide which approach to take, then talk to your friend and address the situation in a way that keeps the friendship strong.
connector_phrases: I've been wanting to talk to you about
It's not a big deal, but
I'm not trying to blame you
I just don't want this to come between us
template_hint: Open in a friendly, non-accusatory way, signal that it's a small but real issue, calmly explain how the lateness affects you using your own feelings, state clearly what you'd like, suggest a practical compromise, and reassure them the friendship matters more than the issue.
sample_response_band12: Hey Sam, can I be honest with you about something small? It's nothing huge, but it's been on my mind. Lately, whenever we make plans, I end up waiting around quite a bit, and the other day I sat at the café for almost forty minutes. I'm not trying to blame you at all, I know life gets hectic, but it does leave me feeling like my time isn't valued, and I didn't want to let that quietly build up. So here's what I'm hoping: if you're running behind, even a quick text would make a huge difference, then I can grab a coffee instead of just sitting there. Honestly, I love hanging out with you, that's the whole point, I just want our time together to feel good for both of us. Are we okay? No hard feelings at all.

title: Returning A Relative's Expensive Gift
prompt_text: A relative has given you a very expensive gift that you feel is far too generous and that you are not comfortable keeping. You worry it may have cost them more than they can afford, and accepting it does not feel right to you. Speak to the relative who gave you the gift. Thank them sincerely and explain, gracefully, why you must return it.
connector_phrases: I'm truly touched that you'd do this, but
Please don't take this the wrong way
It means so much that you thought of me
I just wouldn't feel comfortable keeping it
Your kindness is the real gift
I hope you understand where I'm coming from
template_hint: Open with sincere gratitude, gently explain why the gift feels like too much, return it kindly, and reassure them the gesture itself meant everything.
sample_response_band12: Uncle Sam, I have to talk to you about the gift you gave me, and please don't take this the wrong way, because I'm genuinely touched. When I opened it, I honestly couldn't believe how generous you were. It's a beautiful, beautiful gift. But here's the thing, it's just far too much, and I know it must have cost you a small fortune. As much as I love it, I wouldn't feel right keeping something so extravagant, especially knowing how hard you work. So I'd like to give it back to you, and I really hope you'll understand. The fact that you thought of me this way means more than any present ever could. Honestly, your kindness is the real gift here. Maybe we could just plan a nice dinner together instead? That time with you is what I'd treasure most. I hope that's okay.

title: Ending The Money Lending
prompt_text: A friend has borrowed money from you several times and now wants to borrow again, but you've decided you can no longer keep lending, both for your own finances and for the friendship. You don't want to seem unkind, but you need to set a clear boundary. Call your friend, tell them you can't lend them money anymore, and offer another way to support them.
vocabulary_tips: no easy way to say this
connector_phrases: There's no easy way to say this
I really wish I could, but
I have to look after myself too
What I can do instead is
template_hint: Open kindly and signal this is a hard conversation, acknowledge their situation with empathy, clearly state that you can't lend money anymore and give an honest reason, hold the boundary firmly but warmly, offer a non-financial way to help, and reassure them the friendship is not at risk.
sample_response_band12: Hey Tom, there's no easy way to say this, so I'm just going to be honest because you matter to me. I know things have been tight lately, and I really do feel for you. But I've thought about it a lot, and I can't lend you money this time, and honestly I can't keep doing it going forward either. My own finances are stretched thinner than I've let on, and lending between friends has started to weigh on me too. It's truly nothing personal, it's about looking after both of us. What I can do instead is help you sit down and map out a budget, or look into some support options together, whatever's actually useful. I'm still here for you, that hasn't changed at all. I just can't be the source of loans anymore, and I really hope you understand where I'm coming from.

title: Honest Feedback On Flawed Work
prompt_text: A close friend has spent weeks on an important project and has asked you for honest feedback before they submit it publicly. You can see real problems that could seriously hurt their chances if left unaddressed. You can either give frank, detailed criticism now, or praise the strengths and only gently hint at the issues. Decide which approach to take, then talk to your friend and deliver your feedback in a way that helps without crushing their confidence.
connector_phrases: You asked me to be straight with you, so
There's a lot here that works
I'm telling you this because I believe in it
I really think this can shine
template_hint: Open by genuinely praising the effort and strengths, signal that you're being honest because you want them to succeed, identify the real problems specifically and constructively, frame the issues as fixable, offer to help with the revision, and close by expressing confidence in them.
sample_response_band12: Hey Aisha, thanks for letting me see this before it goes out. You asked me to be straight with you, so I will, because I genuinely want this to do well for you. There's a lot here that works, your core idea is strong and the opening really grabs me. But honestly, the middle section loses focus, and a few key claims aren't backed up yet, and I worry that could make it fall flat with the people you're trying to impress. The good news is, none of this is hard to fix, it's mostly tightening it up and adding a couple of solid examples. If you're up for it, I'd happily sit down this weekend and go through it section by section. I really believe in this project, and with a bit more polish, I think it's going to shine. So, what do you think, want to dig in together?

title: Asking A Friend To Repay
prompt_text: Several months ago you lent a close friend a fairly large sum of money, and they promised to pay you back soon, but they have never mentioned it since. You now genuinely need that money for your own bills, and bringing it up feels awkward because you do not want to seem pushy or damage the friendship. Call your friend, gently remind them about the loan, and ask them to repay you while keeping the conversation friendly and understanding.
connector_phrases: I hate to bring this up, but
template_hint: Open warmly and reassure them this is awkward for you too, then gently remind them of the loan, explain why you now need the money, make a clear and reasonable repayment request, offer a flexible option like instalments, and close by affirming the friendship.
sample_response_band12: Hey Sam, I'm so glad I caught you. Look, this is a little awkward for me, so please bear with me. Do you remember a few months back when I lent you that money to cover your rent? The thing is, I'm a bit tight myself right now, and a couple of bills have just landed, so I was really hoping you could pay me back. I totally get that life's been hectic and it probably slipped your mind, no big deal at all. If paying it all at once is tricky, honestly we could split it into a couple of instalments, whatever works for you. I just wanted to be upfront rather than let it sit awkwardly between us. You mean a lot to me, and I'd never want money to come between us. Let me know what suits you, okay? Thanks so much for understanding.

title: Sorting Out an Uneven Bill
prompt_text: After a group dinner, everyone agreed to split the restaurant bill evenly, but one friend ordered far more food and several expensive drinks while paying the same as everyone else. As the person who collected the money, you have come up short and need to fix it. Speak privately to that friend, tactfully point out the imbalance, and ask them to contribute their fair share without embarrassing them or damaging the friendship.
connector_phrases: I just wanted to have a quick word
I'm sure it wasn't on purpose
If we could just sort that out
template_hint: Pull the friend aside privately, reassure them you are not accusing them, explain the bill came up short because the split was uneven, calmly state how much extra they ordered, ask them to chip in the difference, and close by keeping the tone light and friendly.
sample_response_band12: Hey Jordan, can I grab you for a sec? Nothing serious, I promise. About last night's dinner, I just wanted to have a quick word. Don't get me wrong, I had a great time, but when I added everything up, we ended up a bit short. The way it worked out, we all split it evenly, but you'd gone for the steak and a few cocktails, which bumped the total up quite a lot. I'm sure it totally slipped your mind in the moment, it happens to everyone. Would you mind chipping in maybe twenty extra so we can settle up? That would even things out for the rest of us. Honestly, it's no big deal, I just wanted to be straight with you rather than let it bother me. Thanks so much, Jordan. Let's do it again soon, my treat next time.

title: Cannot Split The Gift Evenly
prompt_text: Your friend group is buying an expensive shared gift for a mutual friend's birthday, and everyone assumed the cost would be split equally. Money is unusually tight for you this month, so paying a full equal share simply is not possible right now. Call one of the organizing friends and explain your situation honestly. Either ask to contribute a smaller amount or suggest a more affordable alternative so you can still be included.
connector_phrases: I feel a bit awkward saying this
I really don't want to miss out
template_hint: Greet the organizer warmly, acknowledge you love the gift idea, honestly explain that money is tight this month, request to contribute a smaller amount or propose a cheaper option, reassure them you still want to be part of it, and thank them for understanding.
sample_response_band12: Hi Priya, thanks for organizing the gift, it's such a lovely idea and I know Maya will adore it. So, I feel a bit awkward saying this, but I wanted to be honest with you. Money's a little tight for me this month, with a few unexpected bills, and I just can't manage the full equal share right now. I really don't want to miss out on being part of the present, though. So I was wondering, would it be okay if I chipped in a smaller amount this time? Or, if that throws off the budget, maybe we could pick something a touch more affordable that everyone can comfortably cover. I'm easy either way, whatever works best for the group. I just didn't want to go quiet and leave you wondering. Thanks so much for understanding, Priya. Let me know what you think.

title: Disputing an Unfair Household Cost
prompt_text: Your roommate has added a large shared household expense to this month's costs and is asking everyone to split it equally, but the charge is mainly for something only they use and benefit from. You feel it is unfair to pay an equal share, yet you live together and want to avoid a tense standoff. Talk to your roommate, calmly explain why the split seems unfair, and propose a more reasonable arrangement while keeping the peace at home.
connector_phrases: I really value living with you
template_hint: Open by saying you want to talk something through calmly, raise the expense without blaming, explain why an equal split feels unfair given who uses it, propose a fairer arrangement such as a usage-based split, invite their view, and reassure them you value the living situation.
sample_response_band12: Hey Chris, can we talk about something for a minute? It's nothing major, I just want to clear the air before it turns into a thing. So, about that new charge you added to this month's bills, I might be wrong, but it doesn't quite seem right to split it evenly. From my side, that subscription is really something you use day to day, and I hardly touch it, so paying a full half feels a bit unfair. I'd feel better if we worked something out together. How about you cover the bulk of that one, and we keep splitting the genuinely shared stuff fifty-fifty as usual? Or we could go usage-based going forward. I'm open to whatever feels fair to both of us. Honestly, I really value living with you, and I don't want money stuff to get awkward between us. What do you reckon?

title: Declining A Relative Moving In
prompt_text: A relative has asked to move into your home temporarily while they sort out their situation. You sympathize with what they are going through, but for personal reasons your home cannot accommodate them right now. Speak to the relative who made the request. Turn down their request kindly and offer to help in another way.
connector_phrases: I really wish I could say yes, but
I hate having to tell you this
Please know it's not about you
Our home just isn't set up for it right now
Here's what I can do instead
I'm in your corner, no matter what
template_hint: Open with empathy for their situation, explain honestly why you can't take them in, decline clearly, then offer a concrete alternative form of help.
sample_response_band12: Hey Marcus, I've been thinking a lot about what you asked, and I want to talk it through with you honestly. First, I'm so sorry you're going through such a rough patch, and it means a lot that you trusted me enough to ask. I really wish I could say yes. The truth is, though, our place just isn't set up for it right now. With the kids in both bedrooms and money being tight, we're stretched about as thin as we can be, and having someone move in just isn't feasible. Please know this isn't about you at all. But I don't want to leave you stranded, so here's what I can do. I'd be glad to help you look for an affordable rental, chip in a bit toward your deposit, and you're always welcome here for dinner anytime. We'll get through this together, okay? I've got your back.

title: Friend Who Never Pays Their Share
prompt_text: Whenever you and a particular friend go out together, they consistently find a way to avoid paying their share, leaving you to quietly cover the difference time after time. It has become a pattern, and although you value the friendship, the situation is starting to bother you and strain your budget. Speak directly to your friend about it: raise the issue honestly without making them defensive, and ask that you split costs fairly from now on.
connector_phrases: I hope you understand where I'm coming from
template_hint: Start by saying you value the friendship and have something honest to share, describe the recurring pattern using 'I' statements rather than blame, explain the impact on your budget, ask to split costs fairly from now on, invite their perspective, and reassure them the friendship matters more than the money.
sample_response_band12: Hey Dani, there's something that's been on my mind, and because I really value our friendship, I'd rather just be honest than let it fester. I've noticed that whenever we go out, I tend to end up covering most of the bill, and over time it's started to add up for me. I'm not saying you do it on purpose at all, I think it just sort of happens. But money's a bit tight for me lately, and quietly picking up the tab is starting to sting a little. So going forward, could we just agree to go halves, or split things properly each time? That way I can keep saying yes to hanging out without stressing about it. I hope you understand where I'm coming from, there's genuinely no hard feelings here. You're one of my favourite people, and I'd hate for something this small to come between us. Sound fair?

title: Splitting A Large Utility Bill
prompt_text: This month's electricity bill has just arrived, and it is far higher than usual, mostly because a couple of your housemates ran the heat and appliances constantly while you were away or barely home. They expect everyone to split it equally, but that does not feel fair given the big difference in usage. Talk to your housemates, explain why an equal split seems unreasonable this time, and suggest a fairer way to divide the cost while keeping things friendly.
connector_phrases: I get that it's tricky to be exact, but
Let me know what you all think
template_hint: Greet the housemates, raise the unusually high bill, acknowledge that splitting bills evenly is normally easy, explain why equal feels unfair this month given the usage difference, propose a fairer split such as usage-based or a middle-ground figure, offer a fallback, invite their input, and keep the tone cooperative.
sample_response_band12: Hey guys, have you all seen this month's electricity bill? It's honestly skyrocketed, way higher than anything we've had before. I wanted to chat about it before we just split it down the middle. I get that it's tricky to be exact with these things, and normally I'm totally happy to go even. But the way I see it, this one's mostly down to the heating and appliances running non-stop, and I was actually away for a big chunk of the month. So splitting it equally feels a little off this time. Could we maybe work out a fairer share, something based roughly on who was home and using stuff? Or, just to keep things fair, we could meet halfway and I'll chip in a bit less than usual. I'm not trying to make a fuss. Let me know what you all think, I'm easy.

title: Cancelling a Last-Minute Appointment
prompt_text: You have a dental check-up booked for this afternoon, but an urgent situation has come up and you can no longer make it. You know the clinic has a strict 24-hour cancellation policy and may charge a fee for late cancellations. Call the receptionist to cancel at short notice, apologise for the inconvenience, and ask to reschedule for another day this week.
template_hint: Greet the receptionist, apologise for the short notice, briefly explain the urgent situation, ask to cancel and reschedule with specific days, acknowledge the policy, and thank them warmly to keep goodwill.
sample_response_band12: Hi, this is Maya Chen calling about my check-up at three o'clock today. I'm so sorry to do this at the last minute, but a family emergency has just come up and there's no way I can make it in this afternoon. I know you have a twenty-four-hour cancellation policy, and I feel terrible about the short notice. Would it be possible to reschedule for later this week instead? I'm pretty flexible, so Thursday or Friday morning would both work really well for me. And if there's any way the cancellation fee could be waived, given that this is genuinely an emergency, I'd be incredibly grateful. I really don't make a habit of this. Thank you so much for being understanding. Just let me know what's available and I'll lock it in right away. I really appreciate it.

title: Rescheduling A Key Client Meeting
prompt_text: You have an important meeting scheduled tomorrow with a major client who flew in specifically to see you, but an urgent conflict means you must change it. You can either offer to meet later the same day, after their other commitments, or push the meeting to a video call next week. Decide which option to propose, then call the client to explain the change and persuade them that it still works in their favour.
connector_phrases: I want to be upfront with you
I'll make sure it's worth your while
template_hint: Open professionally and warmly, signal there's a problem, explain the conflict honestly, present your chosen option with its benefit to the client, acknowledge the inconvenience, and reassure them of your commitment to the relationship.
sample_response_band12: Hi David, thanks for taking my call. I want to be upfront with you because I genuinely value our working relationship. Unfortunately, an urgent issue has landed on my desk that I simply can't move, and it clashes directly with our meeting tomorrow morning. The thing is, I know you flew in specifically for this, so the last thing I want is to waste your trip. What I'd like to propose is meeting later the same day, around five, once your other appointments wrap up. That way we still connect face-to-face while you're in town, and honestly I'll come far better prepared. Dinner's on me afterwards, too. I completely understand if that feels tight, but I promise I'll make it worth your while. Would the later slot work for you? I'm determined to get this right.

title: Telling A Contractor Work Is Unsatisfactory
prompt_text: You hired a contractor to renovate your kitchen, but the finished work has serious problems: uneven tiles, gaps in the cabinets, and paint that is already peeling. The contractor expects the final payment today. Speak to the contractor face-to-face, explain that the work is not acceptable, and insist that the issues be fixed before you pay the remaining balance.
connector_phrases: I have to be honest with you
I'm sure we can sort this out
Before I can pay the balance
I'd really like to make this work
template_hint: Address the contractor by name and acknowledge their effort, calmly state that the work falls short, list the specific defects, firmly link the final payment to fixing them, and offer a cooperative path so the relationship stays professional.
sample_response_band12: Hi Robert, thanks for coming by. I have to be honest with you, and I'd rather say this in person. I really appreciate the hours you've put in, but the finished kitchen just isn't up to the standard we agreed on. The tiles are noticeably uneven near the window, there are visible gaps along the cabinet doors, and the paint is already peeling by the sink. I know you're hoping to settle the final payment today, and I do want to pay you, genuinely. The issue is, I can't release that balance until these problems are properly rectified. I'm not trying to be difficult here. If you can come back and sort out the snag list this week, we're completely square and I'd happily recommend you. I think we can make this work. Can we agree on a day for the repairs?

title: Refunding An Auto-Renewed Subscription
prompt_text: Your annual streaming subscription auto-renewed yesterday for a full year, charging your card $180, even though you had already decided to stop using the service. You never received any reminder that the renewal was coming. Call the company's support line to cancel the renewed subscription and request a full refund, explaining that you have not used the service since it renewed.
connector_phrases: It would mean a lot if
I'd hate to leave on a bad note
template_hint: Greet support, explain the auto-renewal charge, point out you got no reminder and haven't used the service, request cancellation plus a full refund with clear reasons, and close politely while leaving room for goodwill.
sample_response_band12: Hi, thanks for picking up. I'm calling because my annual plan auto-renewed yesterday and charged me a hundred and eighty dollars for the whole year. The problem is, I'd already decided to stop using the service, and I never got any reminder that the renewal was coming up. If I'd had even a quick heads-up email, I absolutely would have cancelled in time. I haven't watched anything since it renewed, so the account is completely untouched. So what I'm hoping is that you can cancel the plan and refund the full amount as a courtesy. I really think that's fair, given there was no notice at all. I've genuinely enjoyed the service over the years, and I'd hate to leave on a sour note over this. Could you process the refund today? I'd really appreciate your help sorting it out.

title: Ending Lessons With A Tutor
prompt_text: You hired a private piano tutor for your daughter, but after three months her progress has stalled and she now dreads the lessons. The tutor is kind and clearly trying hard, which makes this awkward. Speak to the tutor directly, explain honestly that the lessons are not working, and end the arrangement without damaging the relationship.
connector_phrases: Please don't take this the wrong way
I've given this a lot of thought
template_hint: Open kindly and signal the topic is sensitive, sincerely acknowledge the tutor's effort, explain gently and honestly why it isn't working, state your decision to stop with reasons, soften it with appreciation and possibly a referral, and close on a warm note.
sample_response_band12: Hi Sophie, do you have a minute? This isn't easy for me to say, so please don't take it the wrong way. I've given it a lot of thought, and I've decided it's best if we stop the piano lessons for now. It's genuinely nothing to do with you, you've been so patient and kind, and I can see how hard you've tried. The honest truth is that my daughter's progress has stalled, and lately she's been dreading her lessons, which breaks my heart a little. I think it's just not the right fit at this stage, and I'd rather be upfront than let it drag on. I really do appreciate everything you've done with her. I'd be more than happy to recommend you to other parents, and please know there are no hard feelings at all. Thank you so much for understanding.

title: Addressing Your Cleaner's Repeated Lateness
prompt_text: You use a regular house-cleaning service, and the cleaner you've had for months keeps arriving an hour or more late without warning, which throws off your whole schedule. You like her work and don't want to switch providers, but the lateness has become a real problem. Speak directly to her: raise the issue politely, explain how it affects you, and ask her to commit to arriving on time or giving you advance notice going forward.
connector_phrases: I wanted to chat about something
I really value your work, but
It would really help me if
I'm sure we can sort this out
template_hint: Greet warmly and praise the work first, gently raise the lateness with a specific impact, ask clearly for punctuality or advance notice going forward, invite her side, and close by reaffirming you want to keep working together.
sample_response_band12: Hi Carla, do you have a quick second? I wanted to chat about something small. First off, I really value your work, the place always looks fantastic, and that's exactly why I want to keep you on. The thing is, over the past few weeks you've been arriving an hour or more late, and because I work from home, it honestly throws off my whole schedule. I'm not upset at all, I just need us to be on the same page. So going forward, could I ask you to arrive at our agreed time, or at least give me a quick heads-up text if something comes up? Even a five-minute warning would make a big difference. Is everything okay on your end, by the way? I'm sure we can sort this out, and I'd really love to keep working with you long-term.

title: Asking A Teammate To Redo Work
prompt_text: You are working on a group project, and one teammate has finished their section, but the quality is well below what the assignment requires and it will hurt everyone's grade. You need to tell this person that their section needs major rework before submission, without making them feel attacked or making them want to quit the team. Speak directly to your teammate, explain the problem honestly, and persuade them to redo the section while keeping the relationship friendly.
connector_phrases: I really appreciate the work you put in, but
I'm happy to help you with
template_hint: Greet the teammate warmly, acknowledge their effort, gently explain why the section falls short, request the rework with clear reasons, then offer to help and reassure them about the team.
sample_response_band12: Hey Daniel, thanks so much for getting your part done early, I really do appreciate it. Look, I need to be honest with you, and I hope you'll take this the right way. The thing is, after reading your section, I don't think it quite meets what the professor is asking for, especially the analysis part, which feels a bit thin. I'm a little worried because our grades are all tied together, you know? So I was hoping you might be willing to go back and rework it, mainly adding more detail and some sources. Don't get me wrong, the structure is great, it just needs strengthening. And honestly, I'm more than happy to sit down with you this weekend and work through it together. We make a good team, and I know we can pull this off. Sound okay to you?

title: Asking Professor For Extension
prompt_text: You have a major assignment due in two days, but a serious situation at home has made it impossible to finish on time. You know the syllabus clearly states that late work is normally not accepted, yet you genuinely need more time to do the work well. Talk directly to your professor: explain your circumstances honestly and respectfully, and ask for a few extra days while showing that you are still committed to producing quality work.
connector_phrases: I'm really sorry to ask this, but
I want to be upfront with you
template_hint: Address the professor politely, acknowledge the strict deadline policy, explain your genuine situation, make the specific extension request with reasons, and reassure them you remain committed to quality work.
sample_response_band12: Hi Professor Reyes, do you have a quick minute? I wanted to speak with you in person about the research paper that's due Thursday. I'm really sorry to even ask this, because I know the syllabus says late work usually isn't accepted, and I completely understand if it's not possible. The thing is, I've had a serious family emergency this week, and honestly I've fallen behind through no fault of my planning. I want to be upfront with you about that. Would it be possible to have just two or three extra days, so I could hand it in by Sunday? I'd much rather submit something I'm proud of than rush and turn in weak work. I take full responsibility, and I promise I'll make up for it with a strong paper. Thank you so much for hearing me out, it really means a lot.

title: Disputing An Unfair Grade
prompt_text: You received a much lower grade on an essay than you expected, and after reviewing the feedback, you genuinely believe the marking was unfair or based on a misunderstanding of your work. You need to raise this with your professor without sounding defensive or accusing them of making a mistake. Speak directly to your professor, calmly explain why you think the grade should be reconsidered, and politely ask them to take another look.
connector_phrases: I hope you don't mind me asking, but
template_hint: Greet the professor respectfully, acknowledge their authority and your possible misunderstanding, explain calmly why you feel the grade is unfair with specific reasons, request a reconsideration, and thank them while keeping the tone humble.
sample_response_band12: Hi Professor Lin, thanks for taking the time to see me. I wanted to talk about my grade on the last essay, and I really value your feedback, so I hope you don't mind me raising this. I might be wrong here, but from my point of view, the comments say I didn't address the second argument, and I think I actually covered it in detail on the third page. So I'm a little confused about the discrepancy. Could you help me understand where I may have fallen short against the rubric? And if you'd be open to it, would you mind taking another look at that section? I'm honestly not trying to argue the mark; I just want to make sure I understand and learn from it. Either way, I really appreciate you hearing me out. Thank you so much.

title: Choosing To Study Alone
prompt_text: You have been studying with a close friend for months, but lately your sessions have become more social than productive, and your grades are slipping as a result. You have decided you need to study alone from now on, but you do not want to lose the friendship or hurt your friend's feelings. Speak directly to your study partner, explain your decision honestly, and reassure them that this is about your own focus, not about them.
connector_phrases: I've been meaning to talk to you about
Please don't take this the wrong way
It's nothing to do with you
I really hope we can still
I was thinking maybe we could instead
template_hint: Open warmly and value the friendship, acknowledge how awkward the topic is, explain honestly why studying together isn't working for you, state your decision to study alone with reasons, and reassure them by suggesting other ways to stay close.
sample_response_band12: Hey Priya, can we chat for a sec? I've been meaning to bring something up, and please don't take this the wrong way, because I honestly love hanging out with you. The thing is, when we study together lately, we end up talking more than working, and to be honest, my grades have started slipping. So I've decided I need to start studying on my own for a while, just so I can really knuckle down and focus. It's genuinely nothing to do with you, it's more about how easily I get distracted. I really hope we can still grab coffee or have lunch together, because I'd hate to lose that. Maybe we could even meet up after exams to celebrate? I just need to lock in for now. Are we cool?

title: Declining To Share Your Assignment
prompt_text: A classmate who is also a good friend has asked to copy your completed assignment because they ran out of time, but you know that sharing it could get you both in serious trouble for academic dishonesty. You need to refuse without damaging the friendship or coming across as judgmental. Speak directly to your classmate, decline their request firmly but kindly, and offer a different way to help them instead.
connector_phrases: I really wish I could, but
It's not that I don't want to help
What I can do instead is
template_hint: Greet your classmate kindly, acknowledge their stressful situation, explain firmly why you can't share the work and the risk involved, decline clearly, then offer a genuine alternative to help and reassure the friendship.
sample_response_band12: Hey Marcus, I can tell you're really stressed about this deadline, and I honestly feel for you. I really wish I could just hand you my assignment, but the problem is, if I do that and it gets flagged, we'd both be in serious trouble for academic dishonesty. I just can't put us both at risk like that, and I hope you understand it's not that I don't want to help you. It's not about you at all, it's just a line I have to draw. But here's the thing, I'd genuinely love to help another way. What I can do instead is sit with you tonight and walk you through how I approached it, so you can write your own version. We could even start right now if you want. Let's find a way to get this done together, okay?

title: Asking To Leave A Group
prompt_text: You have been placed in a project group that simply is not working out, with constant conflict and members who do not contribute, and your own grade is now at risk. You have decided to ask your instructor to move you to a different group, even though this might create awkwardness with your current teammates. Speak directly to your instructor, explain the situation fairly without trashing your group members, and request the move while showing you are still a willing, hardworking student.
connector_phrases: I wanted to be honest with you about
I've tried everything I can think of
I'm not trying to point fingers, but
Would there be any way to
template_hint: Address the instructor respectfully, acknowledge that asking to switch groups is a big request, explain the situation fairly and without blaming individuals, request the reassignment with reasons, and reassure them you remain a committed student.
sample_response_band12: Hi Professor Okafor, thanks for making time for me. I wanted to be honest with you about how things are going in my project group, because it's reached a point where I'm genuinely worried. I'm not trying to point fingers at anyone, but the group has really struggled to work together, there's been a lot of conflict, and a few of us have been carrying most of the workload. I've tried everything I can think of to smooth things over, honestly, but we seem to be at an impasse. So I wanted to ask, would there be any way to reassign me to a different group? I know that's a big ask, and I'm sorry to put you in that position. I just want a fair shot at doing good work, and I'm more than willing to jump in and contribute wherever you need me. Thank you for understanding.

title: Raising An Unfair Group Grade
prompt_text: Your group received a single shared grade for a project, but you did the large majority of the work while two members contributed almost nothing. You feel the equal grade is unfair, and you want individual contributions to be considered without simply blaming your teammates. Speak directly to your instructor, explain the situation fairly and with evidence, and ask them to reconsider how the grade was distributed.
connector_phrases: I hope this doesn't come across the wrong way
I want to be fair to everyone, but
Would you be willing to consider
template_hint: Greet the instructor respectfully, acknowledge the awkwardness of raising a group-grade issue, explain fairly with concrete evidence why the equal grade feels unfair, request that individual contributions be considered, and close by showing fairness and good faith.
sample_response_band12: Hi Professor Tan, thanks for seeing me. I hope this doesn't come across the wrong way, because I really don't want to blame anyone, but I wanted to talk about how our group grade was given out. To give you some context, I ended up handling most of the research, the writing, and the slides, while a couple of members weren't really able to pull their weight this time. From where I'm standing, the equal grade just feels a bit unfair given how uneven the workload was. I actually kept the message threads and drafts that show who did what, if that would help. Would you be willing to consider individual contributions when finalizing the marks? I'd really appreciate it. I'm not trying to get anyone in trouble; I just want the grade to reflect the effort fairly. Thank you so much for listening.

title: Fixing The Wrong Hotel Booking
prompt_text: You and your friend Daniel booked a hotel together for a weekend trip, but you have just discovered that the reservation is wrong. The room only sleeps one person, and the dates are off by a day. Call Daniel, explain what went wrong, take responsibility, and work out together how to fix the booking before your trip.
connector_phrases: I've got a bit of bad news
Would it work for you if
Let me know what you think
template_hint: Greet Daniel, break the bad news gently, explain exactly what is wrong with the booking, propose one or two practical fixes with reasons, and close by agreeing on a next step together.
sample_response_band12: Hey Daniel, I've got a bit of bad news about our hotel, so don't panic, but we need to sort this out quickly. It turns out the room I booked only sleeps one person, and the dates are off by a day, so we'd arrive before check-in even opens. I feel terrible about the mix-up, that's on me. The thing is, I called the hotel and they said we can switch to a twin room for just a little more and move the check-in to match our flights. I was thinking we split the small difference and lock it in tonight before it sells out. Would that work for you? If you'd rather look at another place nearby, I'm totally open to that too. Either way, let's decide today so we're not scrambling at the last minute. Just let me know what you think.

title: Deciding Who Gets The Good Room
prompt_text: You are on a group trip, and the rental has only one large room with a private balcony and ocean view, while everyone else must share smaller rooms. As the person who booked the place, you have to decide who gets the good room and explain your choice to the others. You can either give it to the couple celebrating their anniversary, or rotate the room so everyone gets a turn. Decide and explain your decision to the group.
connector_phrases: I want to be upfront with you all
I gave this a lot of thought
I know it's not perfect, but
template_hint: Address the group, acknowledge that it is an awkward call, explain the dilemma, state your decision with clear reasons, offer a fair compromise, and reassure everyone that you want it to feel fair.
sample_response_band12: Hey everyone, I want to be upfront with you all about the rooms, because there's only one with the balcony and ocean view, and it wouldn't be fair for me to just grab it. I gave this a lot of thought. Since Maya and Tom are here for their anniversary, I think it'd be really special to give them that room for the first two nights. After that, I'd love for us to rotate so everyone gets a turn with the view before we leave. I know it's not a perfect solution, but it feels like the fairest way to share something only one of us can have at a time. I hope you'll understand the reasoning. And honestly, if anyone feels strongly or has a better idea, please say so. I'd much rather we sort this out now than have anyone quietly feeling left out all week.

title: Asking To Change The Itinerary
prompt_text: You and your travel companion Priya planned a packed itinerary months ago, but now that the trip has started, you feel exhausted and want to slow the pace down. You can either ask to drop the day-trip to the mountains so you can rest, or swap it for something gentler closer to the city. Talk to Priya, explain how you feel, and propose changing the plan without disappointing her.
connector_phrases: Can I be honest with you
I don't want to be a downer, but
I really don't want to let you down
template_hint: Open warmly, gently raise how you're feeling, explain why the current pace isn't working, propose a specific change with a reason, offer a flexible alternative, and reassure her you still want a great trip together.
sample_response_band12: Hey Priya, can I be honest with you? I'm absolutely loving this trip, but I think I've hit a wall, I'm pretty burnt out from all the early starts. I don't want to be a downer, especially since we planned everything so carefully. The thing is, that long mountain day-trip tomorrow is three hours each way, and I'm worried I'll be running on empty for the rest of the week. I was hoping we could either skip it and have a slow morning, or swap it for something gentler, maybe that little café neighbourhood you wanted to see. How would you feel about that? I really don't want to let you down, because I know you were excited about the mountains. If they matter most to you, let's still go and I'll push through. Whatever works best for you, I just want us both to enjoy the rest.

title: Addressing a Friend's Overspending
prompt_text: You and your friend Marcus agreed to a shared travel budget and a common fund for meals and activities, but he keeps adding expensive restaurants and extras that are draining the money fast. You're worried the fund won't last the trip, and it's becoming awkward. Talk to Marcus, raise the overspending tactfully, and work out a way to get the budget back on track without ruining the mood.
connector_phrases: I've been meaning to bring this up
I don't want to make it weird, but
template_hint: Greet your friend, signal it's a delicate topic, calmly lay out the money concern with facts not blame, propose a concrete way to rebalance the budget, suggest a compromise, and reassure the friendship is what matters.
sample_response_band12: Hey Marcus, I've been meaning to bring this up, and I don't want to make it weird, but can we talk about the shared fund for a sec? I've been keeping an eye on it, and we're already over halfway through the money with five days still to go. It's nothing personal, those restaurants have been amazing. I think the issue is the fancy dinners and extras are adding up faster than we planned. Could we maybe do a couple of simpler meals, cook at the place, and save the splurges for one really special night? That way the money stretches and we still treat ourselves. If there's something you really want to do, let's just budget for it on purpose. I value hanging out with you way more than any of this, so let's find a middle ground and keep the trip stress-free. Sound fair?

title: Handling A Missed Connection Together
prompt_text: Your flight was delayed, and your travel partner Sofia has now missed her onward connection, leaving her stranded at the airport while you got through. You have to decide what to do: either stay behind and rebook both of you on a later flight so you travel together, or go ahead as planned and have her catch up to you tomorrow. Call Sofia, explain the situation, and tell her your decision and your reasons. Acknowledge how stressful this is for her and try to keep her on side.
connector_phrases: The reason I say that is
I really don't want to leave you
template_hint: Reach out with concern, acknowledge how stressful it is for her, lay out the two realistic options, state your decision with clear reasons, offer support either way, and reassure her you've got her back.
sample_response_band12: Sofia, I just heard what happened, and I feel awful that you got stuck because of that delay. This is such bad luck. Okay, here's what I'm thinking, and tell me honestly if you disagree. I could come back through and rebook us both on tomorrow's flight, or I push on to the hotel tonight and you join me first thing. The reason I'm leaning toward going ahead is that I can check us in, sort out the rooms, and have everything ready, so you just walk in and relax when you land. I really don't want to leave you on your own, though, so if you'd rather I wait, I'll stay in a heartbeat. Either way, I'll handle the bookings and stay on the phone with you tonight. You're not dealing with this alone. Are you okay with that plan?

title: Leaving The Group Trip Early
prompt_text: You're partway through a week-long trip with friends when something comes up at home, and you've realized you need to leave a few days early. The group built some of their plans around you, and your early departure may affect the shared costs and the final days. Talk to your friends, tell them you have to leave early, and explain how you'll handle the change so they aren't left in the lurch.
connector_phrases: There's something I need to tell you
I'm really sorry to do this
I promise I'll make it up to you
template_hint: Gather the group, signal serious news, apologize and explain why you must leave early, address the practical impact on costs and plans, offer to settle your share fairly, and reassure them you'll make it up.
sample_response_band12: Hey everyone, there's something I need to tell you, and I'm really sorry to do this in the middle of such a great trip. Something's come up at home, and I've realized I need to head back two days early. Believe me, it's not what I wanted, and I feel terrible because I know we planned the last couple of days around all of us being here. I want to be fair, so I'll still cover my full share of the rental and anything we've already paid for. I don't want anyone left in the lurch because of me. For the activities I'll miss, just take me out of the count so you're not splitting my part. I promise I'll make it up to you, dinner's on me when we're all home. I hope you understand, and please, enjoy those last days for me.

title: Sorting Out The Uneven Cost Split
prompt_text: After your group trip, you realize the costs were split unevenly: you covered the rental car, gas, and several group meals, while others paid for very little, yet everyone assumed it all evened out. You can either let it slide to keep the peace, or raise it and ask everyone to settle up fairly. Decide what to do, then talk to your friend Lena, who is organizing the final tally, and explain how you would like to handle it.
connector_phrases: I don't want to make a big deal of it, but
I'm not trying to nickel-and-dime anyone
Whatever feels fair to you all
template_hint: Approach the organizer warmly, downplay any awkwardness, lay out the numbers calmly and factually, state clearly what you'd like, propose a fair way to settle, and reassure them the friendship matters more than the money.
sample_response_band12: Hey Lena, thanks for putting the final numbers together, I know it's a hassle. I don't want to make a big deal of this, but can I flag something before you finalize it? When I added everything up, I realized I'd covered the rental car, most of the gas, and a few of the group dinners, while a couple of people barely chipped in. I think everyone just assumed it evened out, but it didn't quite. I'm honestly not trying to nickel-and-dime anyone. I'd just feel better if we did a quick tally and people squared up their actual share. Would it be possible for you to add my receipts to the spreadsheet and redistribute it? If a perfect split is too messy, I'm happy with whatever feels fair to everyone. The trip was amazing and you guys mean way more to me than the money, I just wanted to be honest with you.

title: Quieting A Library Phone Call
prompt_text: You are studying in the quiet study area of your local library, but the person at the next table keeps taking loud phone calls that make it impossible to concentrate. You would rather sort this out yourself than ask a librarian to step in. Speak directly to this person: politely explain the problem and ask them to take their calls elsewhere or keep them quiet, while staying friendly so things don't become awkward.
connector_phrases: I'm sorry to bother you, but
template_hint: Greet the person gently and acknowledge that calls happen, explain that this is a quiet study area and the noise is breaking your concentration, request that they step outside or speak softly, offer the lobby as a friendly alternative, and thank them warmly to keep the exchange positive.
sample_response_band12: Hi, sorry to interrupt you for a second. I can see you're in the middle of some important calls, and I really don't want to be a nuisance. The thing is, this is the quiet study zone, and I've got a big exam tomorrow that I'm trying to cram for. Your voice carries quite a bit, and I'm finding it really hard to concentrate. I was wondering if you'd mind taking your calls out in the lobby, or maybe just keeping your voice down a little? There's a nice seating area right by the entrance where you can chat freely. I completely understand that you've got things to deal with too, so I really appreciate you being flexible about it. Thanks so much, that's really kind of you.

title: Lowering Music On The Train
prompt_text: You are on a long train ride, and the passenger sitting right beside you is playing music loudly enough that the sound is leaking out of their headphones. You would like some peace and quiet for the rest of the trip. Speak directly to this passenger, explain the situation politely, and ask them to turn the volume down.
connector_phrases: Excuse me, sorry to bother you
template_hint: Excuse yourself politely, acknowledge that they're enjoying their music, explain that the sound is leaking out and it's a long ride, ask them to lower the volume, suggest it might also save their battery, and reassure them you mean no offence.
sample_response_band12: Excuse me, sorry to bother you. I can tell you're really into your playlist, and honestly the music sounds great. The thing is, even with your headphones on, the sound is leaking out quite a bit, and we've still got about two hours left on this ride. I was hoping to rest my eyes for a while, so I was wondering if it'd be possible to turn the volume down just a notch? No offence at all, I just really struggle to switch off with any background noise. As a bonus, it'll probably save your battery for the rest of the trip too. I'd really appreciate you being understanding about it. Thank you so much, and enjoy the rest of your journey.

title: Telling A Stylist The Haircut Is Wrong
prompt_text: You have just had your hair cut, but it is much shorter and quite different from the style you asked for, and you are genuinely unhappy with it. You want to raise this with your hairstylist without being rude, since you may want to come back. Speak directly to the stylist, explain what went wrong, and ask how they can fix it or make it right.
connector_phrases: I don't want to seem ungrateful, but
I'm sure we can sort this out
template_hint: Thank the stylist for their effort, gently acknowledge the awkwardness, explain clearly how the result differs from what you asked for, calmly request a fix or adjustment, suggest a redo or touch-up as a solution, and reassure them you value their work.
sample_response_band12: Thanks so much for your time today, and I really don't want to seem ungrateful. To be honest though, I'm a little taken aback by how it's turned out. What I was picturing was just a light trim with the length kept around my shoulders, but it's come out a fair bit shorter, and the layers are quite different from the photo I showed you. I'm sure there was a bit of a mix-up somewhere. Is there any way we could even out the sides and blend the back a little to soften the shape? I know we can't add length back, but a small touch-up would make a real difference for me. I genuinely like coming here, so I'd really hate to leave on a sour note. I'm confident we can sort this out together. Thank you for understanding.

title: Returning A Wrong Cafe Order
prompt_text: You ordered a specific meal and drink at a busy cafe, but the server has just brought you the wrong dish entirely. You are short on time and want the correct order without causing a scene. Speak directly to the server, point out the mistake firmly but politely, and ask them to bring you what you actually ordered.
connector_phrases: I think there's been a mix-up
template_hint: Get the server's attention politely, acknowledge how busy it is, state clearly that the dish is not what you ordered, calmly ask for the correct item, mention you're a bit pressed for time, and thank them for fixing it quickly.
sample_response_band12: Excuse me, sorry to flag this when you're clearly rushed off your feet. I think there's been a small mix-up with my order. This looks like the grilled chicken sandwich, but what I actually ordered was the veggie wrap with a flat white. I completely understand it's a really busy lunch hour, so no hard feelings at all. The thing is, I'm a bit pressed for time before a meeting, so would you be able to swap this for the right one as soon as you can? I'm happy to wait a few minutes, I just wanted to catch it early before things got even busier for you. Thanks so much for sorting that out, I really appreciate it. The place is lovely, by the way, so I'll definitely be back.

title: Addressing Someone Who Cut In Line
prompt_text: You have been waiting in a long line for nearly twenty minutes when someone steps in directly in front of you as if they had been there all along. You feel this is unfair to you and to everyone behind you. Speak directly to this person, point out that there is a line, and politely ask them to go to the back without starting an argument.
connector_phrases: I'm not sure if you noticed, but
I'm sure it was an honest mistake
template_hint: Politely get their attention, give them the benefit of the doubt that they didn't notice, explain that there's a line and how long you've waited, ask them to join the back, frame it as fairness for everyone, and keep your tone calm and non-confrontational.
sample_response_band12: Excuse me, I'm not sure if you noticed, but there's actually a line here. I've been waiting for nearly twenty minutes, and there are quite a few people behind me too. I'm sure it was just an honest mistake and you didn't see where the line started, that's totally understandable. It's just that it wouldn't really be fair to everyone who's been patiently waiting their turn. Would you mind heading to the back? It's right around the corner, just past those folks by the entrance. I know waiting is a pain, believe me, none of us want to be standing here either. Thanks so much for understanding, I really appreciate you being a good sport about it. Hopefully the line moves a bit quicker for all of us soon.

title: Asking To Share Gym Equipment
prompt_text: You are at a crowded gym and the machine you need has been occupied by the same person for a very long time, and they keep scrolling on their phone between sets. You are on a tight schedule and have to leave soon. Speak directly to this gym member: politely explain your situation and either ask them to finish up soon or propose that the two of you work in together and alternate sets.
template_hint: Greet them in a friendly, non-confrontational way, acknowledge they are mid-workout so you are not being rude, explain that the gym is packed and you are short on time, then propose a solution (either they finish up soon or you work in together and alternate sets), offer to reset the weights so it does not slow them down, and thank them warmly to keep things friendly.
sample_response_band12: Hey, sorry to interrupt your workout. I can see you've still got a few sets to go, and I really don't want to rush you. The thing is, the place is packed today and I'm on a tight schedule, I've only got about twenty minutes before I have to dash. I was wondering, any chance you're nearly done on this machine? Or honestly, would it be alright if we worked in together and alternated sets? I'll happily reset the weight for you each time, so it won't slow you down at all. No worries if you'd rather finish your own thing first, I totally understand, I just figured I'd ask rather than hover awkwardly behind you. Whatever works best for you is fine with me. Thanks so much, I really appreciate it, and enjoy the rest of your session.

title: Asking A Noisy Moviegoer To Stop
prompt_text: You are at the cinema enjoying a film, but the person sitting next to you keeps talking loudly and checking their bright phone. It is ruining the experience for you and the people nearby, and you want to watch the rest of the movie in peace. Speak directly to this person, politely point out the disturbance, and ask them to keep quiet and put their phone away. Try to resolve it without making the situation tense or embarrassing.
connector_phrases: Sorry, I don't mean to be that person, but
template_hint: Lean over and speak quietly, soften it with a touch of humour or humility, explain that the talking and phone glare are distracting, ask them to keep quiet and put the phone away, suggest chatting after the film, and thank them so the rest of the movie stays pleasant.
sample_response_band12: Sorry, I really don't mean to be that person, but would you mind keeping it down a little? It's just that I've been looking forward to this film for ages, and I'm finding it tricky to follow the story with the chatting going on. The phone screen is pretty bright too, and the glare keeps pulling my eyes away from the movie. I'm sure you didn't realize how much it carries in here, it's easily done. Whatever you need to sort out, maybe it could wait until after the show, and we can both enjoy the ending? I'd genuinely appreciate it, and honestly the folks around us would too. Thanks a lot for understanding, that's really decent of you. Right, let's get back to it before we miss the good part. Cheers.

title: Two Friends Need You Now
prompt_text: Two of your close friends have both asked for your help on the same Saturday afternoon. One friend needs help moving into a new apartment, while the other wants you to drive her to an important medical appointment because she is too nervous to go alone. You can only help one of them. Call the friend you cannot help, explain that you have chosen to assist the other person, and try to keep your friendship strong.
connector_phrases: I promise I'll make it up to you
template_hint: Greet the person warmly, acknowledge that you hate to disappoint them, explain that both requests landed at once, state which one you chose and why it was more time-sensitive, offer to help them another way or another day, and reassure them the friendship matters.
sample_response_band12: Hey Maria, I'm so glad you called, but I have to be honest with you about Saturday. The thing is, I was really torn, because Daniel also asked me to drive him to a medical appointment that same afternoon, and he's genuinely terrified to go alone. After thinking it over, I decided I have to be there for that, because missing a doctor's visit could be serious. I feel awful saying no to your move, I really do. But here's what I can do: I'm completely free on Sunday, so I'll come over first thing and help you carry everything and set up your furniture. I could even bring lunch to make up for it. I hope you understand, Maria. You know how much our friendship means to me, and I promise I'll be there for you next time.

title: Scaling Back A Big Promise
prompt_text: You told your sister you would plan and pay for her entire birthday celebration, including a big dinner, a rented venue, and a weekend trip. You now realize you took on far more than your time and budget allow. Call your sister, explain that you have to scale back what you promised, and work out a smaller plan together so she still feels celebrated.
connector_phrases: I have to be honest with you
I got a little carried away
template_hint: Open with warmth, admit you got carried away when you made the promise, explain the time and money reality without over-apologizing, propose a smaller but still special plan, invite her input, and reassure her that the celebration still matters to you.
sample_response_band12: Hi Sofia, I need to talk to you about your birthday, and I want to be honest with you. When I offered to plan and pay for everything, I got a little carried away, because I was so excited for you. The reality is, I've looked at my time and my budget, and I just can't pull off the big venue and the whole weekend trip without stressing myself out. I'm sorry, I really overpromised. But I still want to make this special. How would you feel if we did a lovely dinner at that restaurant you love, just close family, and then a fun day out instead of the trip? I'd happily cover that. The most important thing to me is celebrating you, not the size of the party. Let's plan it together so it feels exactly right for you, okay?

title: Asking A Coworker To Swap
prompt_text: Your manager has assigned you to lead the tedious, time-consuming job of organizing the old storage room this weekend, but you have a long-standing family commitment you cannot move. You need to ask a coworker to take the task on instead. Speak to your coworker, explain the situation honestly, and persuade him to take it while keeping a good working relationship.
connector_phrases: I'm in a bit of a bind
I completely understand if you can't
template_hint: Approach the coworker politely, acknowledge it's a big ask, explain honestly why you genuinely can't do it this weekend, be upfront that the task isn't glamorous, offer a clear trade or favour in return, and thank them while leaving them free to say no.
sample_response_band12: Hey Ravi, do you have a second? I hate to ask this, but I'm in a bit of a bind. The manager put me in charge of clearing out the old storage room this weekend, and honestly, it's going to be a long, tedious job. The problem is, I've had a family commitment locked in for months that I just can't move. Is there any chance you'd be willing to take it on for me? I know it's not the most exciting task, so I wouldn't ask if I had another option. To make it fair, I'd happily cover your next late shift, or take that inventory job you've been dreading. I'd genuinely owe you one. I completely understand if you can't, but it would really save me. Either way, thanks so much for hearing me out, Ravi.
